title: Model-based evidence for task-dependent representations in psychological similarity and working memory
description: A central question in visual working memory (VWM) is what psychological representation underlies performance. Standard measurement models assume recall error spreads over the physical stimulus space (e.g., a von Mises distribution on a color wheel), whereas the Target Confusability Competition model (Schurgin et al., 2020) proposes that error should instead be scaled by psychophysical similarity. Recent replications, however, find little benefit from similarity-based adjustments to VWM models (Oberauer, 2023; Tomić & Bays, 2024). One limitation of this debate is that similarity is often treated as a separate input—measured in an independent task or approximated parametrically—rather than inferred jointly with the cognitive processes that generate behavior. We address this limitation by developing Bayesian generative models that infer latent stimulus representations of oriented lines based on responses in perceptual reproduction, memory reproduction, and similarity comparison tasks (Tomić & Bays, 2024). The inferred representations deviate systematically from physical orientation, but in task-dependent ways: reproduction tasks show a bias towards the vertical meridian, whereas similarity judgments show attraction toward the cardinal orientations. Using Bayesian model comparison, we find that the task-specific representations provide a better explanation than a common-cause model that assumes a shared representation. This indicates mental representations should be modeled as part of task-specific generative processes, not assumed from similarity judgments alone. We conclude by highlighting two future research directions: attempting to unify our task-specific representations with additional cognitive processes, or attempting to catalog families of task-dependent representations by exploring different cognitive process models.

title: Empathy shapes multimodal child-directed communication and children’s language comprehension
description: Effective communication requires speakers to tailor what they say, and how they say it, to their audience. Empathy may support this process, but it remains unclear whether empathic speakers adapt multimodal signals (speech, prosody, and gesture) in ways that benefit recipient understanding. We examined this question in child-directed communication. In Experiment 1, 46 broadcast-trained Chinese speakers produced semi-spontaneous programmes for child and adult audiences. Higher-empathy speakers showed stronger child-directed adaptation across lexical–syntactic, prosodic and gestural channels, including more audience-oriented lexical choices, enhanced pitch modulation, and more frequent and salient representational gestures. In Experiment 2, 221 children (7–11 years) viewed a subset of these materials from Experiment 1 for a comprehension task. Children understood child-directed programmes better when they were produced by higher-empathetic speakers, whereas empathy-related differences were minimal for adult-directed programmes. Cross-experimental mediation analyses showed that stimulus-level multimodal cues accounted for part of the association between speakers’ empathy and children’s comprehension. This indirect association was positive in child-directed programmes but negative in adult-directed programmes, indicating that empathy benefits children’s comprehension when speakers’ adaptations are aligned with children’s needs. These findings suggest that empathy operates as a higher-order communicative capacity, shaping audience-specific multimodal behaviour with consequences for children’s understanding.

title: Researchers in the behavioural and brain sciences can use e-values to safely optionally stop during data collection
description: This paper is about a central problem facing researchers in the behavioural and brain sciences: limited resources constrain the amount of data we can collect. To address this familiar concern, we describe a new method for monitoring evidence during data collection to prevent wasted effort and resources. An emerging statistical framework called safe statistics, or anytime-valid inference, provides a statistically valid way to optionally stop (or continue) data collection. Before we describe this new framework, we briefly consider a few existing alternatives aimed at solving the same problems including group sequential designs and sequential Bayesian statistics. We point to other reviews of these approaches and discuss some reasons why uptake among behavioural and brain scientists has been limited. We then make the case that the safe statistics framework is well-suited to some of the common challenges that researchers face. We show how the framework works in a set of case studies and discuss its current limitations. We also provide code researchers can use from the safestats R package along with a companion shiny application to help the interested reader get started.

title: Comments on “Animal personality is a class of variation not a set of traits” by Réale, Dall, and Dingemanse (2026). Praise, and a pathway for integrating the study of personality evolution
description: Réale, Dall, and Dingemanse have contributed a valuable commentary, both in terms of clarifying how to (and not to) consider “personality traits” and in, perhaps unknowingly, providing a glimmer of a bridge that can unify the study of animal and human personality. My comments reveal the bridge in all its glory.

title: Inference of light-source positions and of transparency in brightness illusion displays (Digital Typeset Edition)
description: Although many researchers have attempted to explain brightness illusions, no consensus agreement has yet been reached.  I here report that two factors are essential to the study of brightness illusions, and propose a Helmholtzian explanation for the illusions based on differences of logical interpretations for the displays.  The factors that there are: (1) a possibility that our visual system unconsciously infers three light-source positions for segments in the displays (the front of the segments, the segments themselves, and the area behind the segments) and (2) a possibility that our visual system interprets a non-transparent achromatic segment not only as a non-transparent surface illuminated from the side, but also as a transparent segment illuminated from behind, a shade, a cast shadow, a self-luminous segment, or a space.  If our visual system take the factors into account and selects the more likely interpretations out of possible interpretations, it is unsurprising that segments that are physically identical gray is perceived as a different brightness in each situation.

title: The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic Symptomatology: A Retrospective Online Study
description:
Background: Previous research has shown that interpersonal trauma is associated with higher rates of posttraumatic stress symptoms, with coping responses playing a vital role in individual differences in symptom presentation. The present online study examined differential associations between trauma type, coping styles, and trauma-related symptomatology to deepen understanding of variation in trauma-related symptomatology.
Methods: The sample included N = 300 participants who reported a traumatic life event within the past 18 months, assessed with the Life Events Checklist for DSM-5 (LEC-5). Events were classified into interpersonal and non-interpersonal trauma. Participants completed retrospective questions assessing posttraumatic coping strategies, current levels of posttraumatic stress symptoms, depression, anxiety, stress, and life satisfaction.
Results: The present study reports secondary analyses. Correlational analyses within both trauma groups revealed positive relationships between avoidant coping and trauma-related symptoms. Group comparisons revealed higher levels of trauma-related symptoms, lower life satisfaction, and greater use of avoidant coping strategies in the interpersonal trauma group compared to the non-interpersonal trauma group. Furthermore, avoidant coping explained a significant proportion of variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms within both trauma groups, and trauma type itself accounted for significant unique variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms across the entire sample. Finally, exploratory mediation analyses provided evidence consistent with a partial mediation of the association between trauma type and posttraumatic stress symptoms.
Conclusions: These findings suggest that avoidant coping may be an important factor in understanding differences in trauma-related symptoms across trauma types. The results also point to potential intervention and prevention targets that should be evaluated in longitudinal research.

title: Principled Default Priors for Bayesian Hypothesis Testing in Binomial GLMs:   A Matched-Prior Framework with Empirical Evaluation
description: Researchers using Bayesian hypothesis testing in binomial generalized linear models (GLMs; for instance, logistic regression for accuracy data) need priors on regression coefficients, but no widely adopted default framework exists. The nonlinear link function creates a prior-link interaction: priors that appear reasonable on the coefficient scale can imply implausible distributions on the probability scale. We show that this problem has an exact solution for CDF-based link functions: matched priors – prior families whose CDF mirrors the link function – produce uniform distributions on the probability scale for the intercept, a maximally uninformative anchor. We extend this family choice to effect priors for parsimony, with prior scale determined via prior predictive simulation. We embed this matched-prior principle in a three-step workflow and evaluate it via simulation: (1) identify matched priors, (2) evaluate prior predictive distributions, (3) calibrate Bayes factors and compare to the JZS/Cauchy default. For logistic regression, the matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior and matched Logistic effect priors with scale 0.25 to 0.30 provide well-calibrated Bayes factors. A signal detection analysis shows that prior scale is a criterion parameter: it shifts the operating point on the ROC curve but does not change discriminability. The advantage of the matched-prior workflow is principled scale selection on the probability scale, where effect sizes are directly interpretable. The workflow extends naturally to hierarchical models, where the prior on the random-effects variance has negligible influence on Bayes factors for fixed effects. Ready-to-use brms code and scaling formulas for any contrast coding are provided.

title: Effects of Cognitive Warmup on Cognitive Performance
description: Cognitive performance is composed of a relatively stable cognitive ability (trait) and transient factors (state). While the trait can be influenced by long-term training, transient factors can be manipulated to achieve short-term improvements. Acute physical exercise has shown to improve subsequent cognitive performance through a warm-up effect; however, it remains unclear whether a similar enhancement can be achieved through cognitive activity itself. The present study investigated whether a brief cognitive warm-up could influence subsequent performance on a neuropsychological measure of processing speed. Forty healthy young adults participated in two experimental sessions and were assigned either to a cognitive warm-up group or a no-warm-up control group. The warm-up consisted of a brief series of serious games targeting executive functions and attention. Cognitive performance was assessed using the Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT). Bayesian analyses indicated an interaction between session and group, with better results for the warm-up group in the active session with respect to the control group, net of scores in Raven’s Matrices. These findings provide preliminary evidence that a brief cognitive warm-up may enhance subsequent performance on a speeded cognitive task. Importantly, the effect emerged in the absence of task-specific priming and did not rely on physical exercise, suggesting that cognitive performance may be influenced through transient modulation of cognitive state. Further research employing active control conditions, larger samples, and direct measures of underlying mechanisms is needed to establish the robustness and specificity of this effect.

title: Working with Circular Data: A Tutorial for Cognitive and Behavioral Research
description: Introductory statistics courses and conventional methods in behavioral and cognitive science are typically restricted to Euclidean space without explicitly stating this, leaving many researchers unaware of how to handle data with circular or periodic properties (e.g., time of day or orientation on a screen). This tutorial provides an accessible yet rigorous introduction, designed to bridge the gap between statistical theory and practical application. After discussing different ways of representing circular data mathematically and fundamental principles for working with them, we explain angular and vector representations and operations, including key considerations for implementing them in computer code. We then survey methods for visualizing circular data, including linear and polar plots and presentation of summary statistics. Next, we introduce circular measures of central tendency, dispersion, and shape: how they relate to their Euclidean counterparts, and why they differ. Finally, we turn to more advanced topics of the kind needed to construct models of circular data. We introduce the most common circular distribution families, including circular analogs of the Gaussian distribution and their properties, as well as flexible distribution families that can describe skewed and heavy-tailed data. We survey common applications in the supplementary materials. The tutorial takes a method-oriented approach, not tied to specific software, but highlighting factors relevant to experimental design, data analysis, and statistical modeling. A consistent theme is the need to rigorously account for the inherent periodicity of circular data. We also provide a consistent notation for circular statistics and parameters, addressing the lack of established conventions in the field.

title: What is critical metascience and why is it important?
description: Metascience uses a scientific approach to understand and improve science. Critical metascience takes a step back to question metascience’s commonly-accepted assumptions, methods, problems, and solutions. In this article, I define critical metascience and explain how it can help to identify and address collective biases within metascience. I also highlight some emerging themes in the field. Finally, I consider some potential relationships between metascience and critical metascience and argue that the most functional model is one in which two fields are partially overlapping. I conclude that there needs to be greater recognition and dialogue between metascience and critical metascience in order to improve metascience’s objectivity and approach.

title: Multi-System Brain-Body Mechanisms of Mental Health in Long-Term Meditation: Toward a Science of Advanced Meditation
description:
Most research on meditation and mental health has focused on short-term interventions in
novice populations, leaving the cumulative effects of long-term and advanced practice
underexplored. Advanced meditation is defined as states, stages, and endpoints that arise with the
maturation and mastery of mental-activity skills. This systematic review synthesizes evidence from
52 studies across four databases, examining mental health outcomes through an integrative brain–
body lens.
Six interrelated domains emerged: (1) well-being, (2) interpersonal functioning, (3) emotion
regulation, (4) sensory–affective uncoupling of pain, (5) stress resilience, and (6) psychiatric
symptom prevention. Rather than operating independently, these domains reflect a coordinated,
multi-system adaptive response. Long-term meditation is consistently linked to enhanced
attentional control, reduced emotional reactivity, and increased compassion, changes supported by
functional and structural neuroplasticity in default-mode, salience, and frontoparietal networks.
Emerging evidence further suggests that advanced practitioners may exhibit qualitatively distinct
neural patterns, including reduced self-referential processing and concentration-related states.
Evidence points to benefits beyond the brain. These neural changes are paralleled by
systemic adaptations: modulation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenocortical axis,
downregulation of inflammatory gene expression, and upregulation of neuroplasticity-related
molecular markers.
Together, these findings support a holistic framework in which sustained meditation
strengthens interconnected neural, endocrine, immune, and cellular systems that collectively
support mental health and resilience. Meditation is therefore best understood not as a purely
cognitive technique but as a systemic brain–body intervention. Future research should prioritize
longitudinal, mechanistic designs and move beyond duration-based definitions toward skill-based
characteriza

title: What is Evaluative Conditioning Anyway? A Response to De Houwer (2026)
description: We respond to De Houwer's (2026) commentary on our preprint (Corneille et al., 2026). In that preprint, we proposed a new definition of the Evaluative Conditioning effect (ECe) to address what we see as limitations in how ECe is currently conceptualized and studied. In the present response, we discuss substantive agreements and disagreements with De Houwer's commentary and clarify apparent misunderstandings on both sides. In doing so, we argue that our proposed definition withstands De Houwer's counter-arguments and offers a sensible alternative to existing definitions. More broadly, we believe that this exchange highlights important conceptual, methodological, and measurement-related questions whose resolution may have far-reaching implications for EC research. We hope that the discussion of these issues lives on beyond this exchange and helps move the field forward.

title: What Makes a Habit? Investigating Potential Determinants of Habit Formation
description: Habitual behaviors are automatic responses to specific cues, acquired through repetition in stable contexts. While habits can support long-term behavior change, relatively little is known about which factors influence their development. We conducted a preregistered reanalysis of a six-month longitudinal study on habit formation during the COVID-19 pandemic. Participants (N = 254) completed monthly surveys assessing behavioral automaticity (i.e., habit) and ten potential determinants. Cluster analyses revealed two trajectories: habit formation and habit degradation, with cluster membership differing by behavioral complexity, frequency, and context stability. Multilevel models showed that six determinants were significantly associated with habit strength: autonomous motivation, behavior frequency, context stability, feeling energized, feeling pleased (positive), and perceived difficulty (negative). A significant interaction between time and behavior frequency emerged after correcting for multiple testing. Findings underscore the dynamic nature of habits and suggest that tailored interventions may be especially important during early formation and to prevent later degradation.

title: Exploring diagnosis of Developmental Coordination Disorder and  Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function: a secondary data analysis using Connected Bradford
description: Background: Approximately 5% of children are affected by a neurodevelopmental disorder of their sensorimotor skills. DSM-V and ICD-10, the two most widely used classifications systems, define this diagnostically as either ‘Developmental Coordination Disorder’ (DCD) or ‘Specific Developmental Disorder of Motor Function’ (SDDMF, respectively). Being diagnosed with DCD/SDDMF has been found to have a detrimental impact on long-term health and education outcomes. Research repeatedly highlights parental dissatisfaction with the pathways for assessment and support of DCD/SDDMF, however, there has been a lack of research quantitatively exploring the diagnostic landscape. This study therefore aimed to evaluate the incidence of DCD/SDDMF in a diverse District (Bradford, UK) from 1950 -December 2024. Methods: Clinical codes related to sensorimotor skill assessments and diagnoses were searched for within the Connected Bradford dataset, which houses routinely collected primary and secondary healthcare data for over 800,000 residents. Results: There are 182,978 children currently in the Connected Bradford primary care records, suggesting there should be ~9149 children with a diagnosis (given estimated prevalence rates of 5%). But over 74 years of records available, there were only 152 recorded childhood diagnoses of SDDMF (the ICD-10 used in the UK and Europe for diagnosis). When exploring related diagnoses and findings there were 1037 cases (~14 per year), however this amount over 74 years is still nine times lower than would be expected within current childhood populations in Bradford. Moreover, there were low levels of recorded assessments and a lack of clarity regarding how children are being assessed. Conclusion: Diagnosis is the current pathway to accessing support across UK services, yet the current study highlights that children with DCD/SDDMF are not being diagnosed within Bradford. This likely reflects national and international challenges. Further research is needed to

title: Behavioral biomarker reliability can mislead mechanistic inference in precision psychiatry
description: Psychological tasks are increasingly used to develop behavioral biomarkers for precision psychiatry, yet poor test-retest reliability of many task-derived summary measures has raised doubts about their use for stratifying patients, predicting treatment response or indexing clinically relevant mechanisms. In simulations of a fear-conditioning task, we establish two results. First, common summaries can mislead in two ways: they can appear unreliable because they are shaped by experimental context, such as trial order, or reliable because they capture stable but unintended features of responding rather than the target mechanism. Second, computational-model parameters can support stronger reliability claims when they more closely track the intended process, provided the model captures the underlying mechanism and the design makes it recoverable. Together, these findings show that reliability can systematically mislead mechanistic inference unless interpreted with respect to a generative model, the diagnosticity of the task design and assumptions linking the metric to the intended mechanism.

title: Diving into the Stigma Around Consensual Non-Monogamies: Comparing Psychopathology, Well-Being, Attachment, and Other Psychological Characteristics with Monogamous Counterparts
description:
Objectives: This study aims to empirically challenge pervasive stigma and stereotypes around Consensual Non-Monogamy (CNM) by comprehensively comparing the psychological profiles of CNM practitioners and monogamous individuals, examining both stigmarelated factors and psychological resources, while exploring the interactive role of LGBTQIA+ identity.
Method: Using a cross-sectional design, 815 Spanish adults (388 CNM, 427 monogamous) completed validated self report measures for all target constructs, with group comparisons conducted via one-way ANCOVAs controlling for age and LGBTIQA+ status.
Results: Given the small effect sizes observed, interpretations are made with caution. Analyses revealed no robust overall differences in psychopathology or dark personality traits, directly refuting maladjustment stereotypes. CNM practitioners reported significantly higher scores in sexual sensation seeking, resilience, autonomy, and personal growth, alongside lower personal distress. Importantly, interaction analyses indicated that LGBTIQA+ monogamous individuals exhibited the highest levels of anxious attachment and psychopathology. Relationship style interacted significantly with LGBTQIA+ identity across several outcomes
Conclusions: Overall, the findings do not support deficit-based accounts portraying CNM as inherently associated with greater psychological risk. Instead, the pattern observed in this sample was largely one of psychological similarity, with some small differences favoring CNM participants on selected resource-related variables. These results support the need for nuanced, evidence-based, and stigma-free frameworks in research and clinical practice addressing diverse relational orientations.

title: Curriculum learning for complex tasks
description: Learning to solve a complex task involves discovering new concepts and understanding how to use them. Here, we studied how these two aspects of learning should be sequenced to promote effective task acquisition. We designed video games in which different concepts (tools or ingredients) were used sequentially to complete the task (reach a goal or create a potion). During self-directed learning, participants chose to progress via a curriculum that graduated the complexity of concept discovery (number of concepts involved) rather than of concept implementation (number of steps to goal). However, those who chose to graduate the implementation complexity performed better. Three subsequent experiments that randomised participants into curriculum groups confirmed that adjusting the complexity of concept implementation (not discovery) is the more effective learning strategy. When acquiring complex tasks, learners should not attempt to discover new concepts prematurely, but instead focus on practicing those which they have already mastered.

title: A Developmental Map of Pathogen-Avoidance Behaviors
description:
Despite the significant costs of early-life infections, young children show little behavioral avoidance of pathogens. This phenomenon raises as-of-yet unanswered questions about when anti-pathogen psychology develops. The present cross-sectional study examined parent-reported observations of 39 behaviors from infancy through middle childhood, based on reports from 3,050 parents from the UK and US. Across fine-grained monthly age intervals (0–120 months), distinct developmental trajectories were identified and then grouped into four clusters based on temporal patterns. Behaviors that increased pathogen exposure—such as mouthing and contact with dirty objects—were most frequent during infancy and toddlerhood. Hygiene routines and disgust responses emerged later and remained stable across the observed age range. Two age-related transitions, around the second and fourth years, marked shifts from early exploratory and risk-prone behaviors toward increasingly organized pathogen-avoidant ones. Together, these findings provide a developmental overview of anti-pathogen psychology and clarify when children begin to act in ways that reduce pathogen exposure.

Keywords: pathogen avoidance; development; disgust; hygiene; behavioral immune system

title: Diving into the stigma around consensual non-monogamies: Comparing psychopathology, well-being, attachment, and other psychological characteristics with monogamous counterparts
description:
Objectives
This study aims to empirically challenge pervasive stigma and stereotypes around Consensual Non-Monogamy (CNM) by comprehensively comparing the psychological profiles of CNM practitioners and monogamous individuals, examining both stigma-related factors and psychological resources, while exploring the intersectional role of LGBTQIA+ identity. 
Method
Using a cross-sectional design, 815 Spanish adults (388 CNM, 427 monogamous) completed validated self-report measures for all target constructs, with group comparisons conducted via one-way ANCOVAs controlling for age and LGTBIQA+ status. 
Results
Given the small effect sizes observed, interpretations are made with caution. Analyses revealed no robust overall differences in psychopathology or dark personality traits, directly refuting maladjustment stereotypes. CNM practitioners reported significantly higher scores in sexual sensation seeking, resilience, autonomy, and personal growth, alongside lower personal distress. Importantly, intersectional analyses indicated that LGTBIQA+ monogamous individuals exhibited the highest levels of anxious attachment and psychopathology, suggesting CNM may buffer these negative outcomes within the LGBTQIA+ community. 
Conclusions
These findings challenge deficit-based narratives, indicating that CNM is not associated with elevated psychological risk but is linked to psychological strengths. This underscores the necessity for nuanced, evidence-based, and stigma-free frameworks in both research and clinical practice addressing diverse relational orientations.

Keywords: non-monogamy; LGTBIAQ+, polyamory; psychopathology; adult attachment; well-being

title: Falling on Fertile Ground: National Identity Rhetoric Mobilises Those High in National Narcissism
description: Politicians frequently talk about national identity and commitment to national interests. We examined whether such rhetoric mobilises supporters by exploring the role of national narcissism—a belief that one’s nation is exceptional and entitled to special treatment. We tested whether national identity rhetoric boosts the audience’s national narcissism or whether it appeals to those already high in national narcissism. In six experiments, conducted in the US and UK (Ntotal = 3,716), we measured reactions to AI-generated political speeches (Studies 1, 3, 4 and 6) and social media posts (Studies 2 and 5). We found that participants high in national narcissism perceived messages promoting national identity as more persuasive and were more willing to share them on social media, compared to messages rejecting national identity (Studies 1 and 2) or neutral ones (Studies 3 and 4). We also found that national narcissism predicted voting for (Studies 1 and 3), and broadly supporting (Studies 4 and 6), leaders who appealed to national identity in their speeches, compared to those who did not. In Study 5, we corroborated our findings by relying on behavioural indices of engagement with social media posts on a mock platform. Similar effects were observed for messages promoting national identification and national narcissism. Furthermore, in Study 6, national narcissism predicted support for leaders promoting (vs. rejecting) national narcissism regardless of their partisan affiliation (as Republican vs. Democrat). Across the six studies, we found no reliable evidence that national identity messages affected participants’ own national sentiments.

title: MEMORY REACTIVATION ALTERS THE FATE OF NEUTRAL EPISODIC MEMORIES FOLLOWING A SUBSEQUENT INDEPENDENT EXPERIENCE
description: Memory reactivation may lead to strengthening, weakening, or updating depending on the experience that follows retrieval. Although post-reactivation modification has been shown for emotional, aversive, and naturalistic episodic memories, it remains unclear whether a consolidated neutral memory for an everyday-like event can be distorted when reactivation is followed by a subsequent independent episodic experience. Yet memories that later become relevant for testimony often initially concern neutral everyday events, such as conversations, routine encounters, or the presence of a person in a given context. Here, we examined whether a consolidated complex neutral episodic memory could be modified when reactivation was followed by a subsequent independent episodic experience. Participants encoded a neutral audiovisual story and returned one week later for an incomplete reminder designed to reactivate the original memory. After reactivation, one group encoded a second neutral audiovisual story, whereas another group received the reminder only. Two control groups either encoded the second story without prior reactivation or received no reminder. One week later, memory was assessed using free recall, cued recall, recognition, and temporal order tasks. Reactivation followed by the second episodic experience reduced accurate recall of the original story and increased false recall and intrusions from the subsequent story. In contrast, reminder-only reactivation was associated with better preservation and fewer distortions. These effects were expressed primarily in free recall. Thus, the fate of a consolidated complex neutral episodic memory depended on post-reactivation experience, with reactivation alone associated with relative preservation and reactivation followed by a new independent episode promoting updating/interference.

title: The case of Michelle: Solutions of best sport psychology practice using four psychological perspectives
description:
Practitioners in sport psychology have long emphasized the importance of intervention plans informed by diverse philosophical and psychological perspectives. However, few publications present practical models drawn from various schools of thought. This paper addresses that gap by presenting responses developed within an international intervision group of sport psychology practitioners, who meet monthly to exchange best practices. We focus on the case of Michelle, a 16-year-old elite gymnast who sustained a serious injury requiring surgery and a prolonged recovery before returning to training and competition. To support Michelle’s rehabilitation, we outline four distinct intervention approaches: the Integrative Approach, the Mindfulness- and Acceptance-based Approach, the Psychodynamic Approach, and the Existential Approach. Each framework provides structured, theory-consistent methods and assessments tailored to the athlete’s physical and psychological needs during recovery. These approaches highlight distinct pathways to enhance resilience, foster motivation, and support reintegration into sport. We aim to inspire young practitioners to select approaches that align with their academic and clinical training, while also encouraging experienced professionals to expand their repertoires beyond habitual methods. As an intervision group, we further aspire to contribute ongoing best-practice recommendations that simplify daily challenges in a demanding and often isolated profession.

Key words: Sport Psychology, approaches, injury, international intervision group

title: Nature immersion in virtual reality does not reduce naturally occurring muscle pain or improve motor outputs during prescribed or self-regulated tasks
description: Virtual reality (VR) can reduce experimental and clinical pain, yet it remains unclear whether these effects translate to naturally occurring muscle pain (NOMP) experienced during physical tasks. This study examined whether nature immersion presented through immersive VR attenuates NOMP and enhances motor performance during prescribed and self-regulated exercise. Two within-participant crossover studies were conducted. In Study 1, twelve healthy participants completed a stepped incremental cycling task to exhaustion under three conditions: immersive VR (nature scene), a two-dimensional sham display, and a no-intervention control. Pain onset, NOMP intensity, perceived effort, and time to exhaustion were measured. In Study 2, twenty-four participants performed intermittent isometric handgrip contractions at fixed light (13/100) or strong (50/100) perceived effort under the same conditions, with self-regulated force output and NOMP intensity measured. Immersive VR did not significantly delay pain onset, prolong time to exhaustion, reduce NOMP intensity, or lower perceived effort compared with sham or control conditions. A small reduction in NOMP intensity occurred early in the cycling task, but effects were trivial and not sustained. In Study 2, NOMP intensity, force production, and fatigue-related declines were similar across conditions. Exploratory analyses showed no moderating effects of sex, environmental presence, or fatigue. Overall, these findings demonstrate that nature-based VR immersion has limited effects on NOMP or motor performance. This likely reflects a mismatch between central mechanisms targeted by VR and strong peripheral drivers of NOMP. Consequently, VR hypoalgesia appears pain-type dependent and potentially pain-intensity dependent, with effects limited to lower pain intensities.

title: Communicative Milestones: Modeling Age of Attainment in the First two Years
description: Early communicative milestones, such as first words and gestures, emerge within the broader context of overall child development. However, because studies typically focus on individual milestones and assess them cross-sectionally, they capture whether or not a milestone has already been achieved, but not when it first emerges — the Age of Attainment (AoA). To address this gap, we analyzed longitudinal data from 4,359 children collected through the Pebbles App, a smartphone-based developmental diary, in which respondents report whether and when a milestone was first observed. This approach provides fine-grained data and allows us to study the underlying dimensionality of the AoA of 14 early communicative milestones within the first two years of life. Exploratory factor analysis revealed a three-factor structure: (1) babbling (e.g., reduplicated babbling), (2) early nonverbal communication (e.g., pointing), and (3) linguistic productivity and gestures (e.g., producing first words). These factors were interrelated, which suggests that very early milestones (e.g., babbling) serve as markers that predict later communicative development within the first two years. The factor structure was invariant across different contextual groups (e.g., sex, caregiver education, or preterm status). Our results provide an important framework for understanding early communicative trajectories through AoA.

title: Prefrontal development during late childhood shapes adolescents’ emotional responses to everyday social rejection
description: Social rejection is a critical risk factor for adolescents’ mental health, yet adolescents differ markedly in how emotionally they respond to everyday rejection. This preregistered study examined adolescents’ emotional responses to everyday social rejection, and whether these responses are moderated by neurodevelopmental changes across childhood and cognitive emotion regulation (CER). Dutch adolescents (N=195, aged 15–17) completed a 17-day Experience Sampling Method (ESM) protocol with six assessments per day, reporting perceived social rejection and momentary affect (loneliness, irritation, cheerfulness) across offline and online social contexts. Longitudinal changes in dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) activation across childhood and CER were assessed as moderators of within-person rejection–affect associations. Daily social rejection was prevalent but typically low in intensity. When adolescents experienced more rejection than usual, they reported increased loneliness and irritation and decreased cheerfulness. These associations were stronger for offline than online rejection and were amplified when rejection occurred across both contexts. Importantly, maladaptive CER strengthened rejection-related loneliness, whereas stronger developmental increases in DLPFC activation across childhood attenuated adolescents’ loneliness responses to rejection. Our findings show that the emotional impact of everyday social rejection reflects individual differences in neurodevelopmental maturation and highlight emotion regulation as a potential mechanism underlying adolescents’ social sensitivity.

title: Unlocking the potential of simulation studies in network psychometrics: A tutorial
description: Simulation studies are a powerful tool within the field of network psychometrics, providing insight into sample size determination and best practices for achieving accurate results. However, the advantages simulation studies provide have thus far been predominately harnessed by psychological researchers with a statistical or methodological background. While user-friendly methods for simulating psychological networks have been developed, there remains a lack of comprehensive guidelines for their effective utilization. In response, this tutorial paper serves as a guide on how to set up simulation studies for three widely used network modeling techniques: the Ising model, the Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM), and the Graphical Vector Autoregressive (GVAR) model. We explain the rationale behind simulation studies and present a step-by-step roadmap to initiate one's own simulation studies. This roadmap covers all essential aspects using a practical example to guide the reader. In addition, we provide an overview of open-source software packages available for simulating network models in the statistical programming environment R, and demonstrate their practical application. In doing so, we aim to enable more researchers to explore and resolve statistical questions within network psychometrics.

title: A spatiotemporal hierarchy for social interaction perception in the lateral visual stream
description: The lateral visual stream has been recently proposed as a third visual stream, in addition to the ventral and dorsal streams, specialized for processing dynamic social content. While prior work has suggested that the regions of this pathway form a hierarchy representing increasingly abstract information, the computations along this pathway are still largely unknown. High spatiotemporal resolution data are particularly informative for characterizing the information flow and thus neural computations across different brain regions. Using a novel regression approach, we combine data from EEG, fMRI, and behavior in response to the same videos to leverage the high temporal resolution of EEG and whole-brain spatial resolution of fMRI. We find that low-level visual features are represented in early visual cortex with a short temporal latency and are not represented in higher-level regions of the lateral stream. Further, we find that mid-level features are represented in mid-level lateral regions with a shorter latency than high-level features in more anterior regions of the lateral pathway. However, both mid- and high-level features were decodable in anterior regions of the lateral pathway with a similar latency. Together, these results provide evidence that features of social actions are processed rapidly in the lateral visual stream in a manner that is consistent with hierarchical processing, but the lateral stream does not exhibit a strict temporal sequence of representational transformations along the posterior-to-anterior axis.

title: Do Control Input Metrics Capture Game Feel and Skill-Challenge Balance?
description:
Game feel - virtual sensation during the real-time control of virtual objects - is considered a central element of game design. A growing body of research investigates its impact on player experience. However, questions remain regarding how to study and analyse game feel, complicating evaluation and impeding our understanding of this phenomenon. Drawing from ecological psychology, we report on an experimental study to understand whether measures used in the study of skilled movement have potential in characterising game feel.
We find that metrics derived from player control input distinguish between player skill levels and different kinds of game-feel in the same game. Among these, multifractality - a measure of complex nesting in player control movements - shows sensitivity to skill-challenge balance. We discuss the relationship of these measures to player experience and game feel experience. Collectively, our findings point to more fine-grained approaches to capturing game feel related to real-time control.

title: Acoustic Startle Reactivity and Pre-Pulse Inhibition in ADHD and Autism: Evidence for a Developmental Emergence of Group Differences
description: The acoustic startle response (ASR) and its modulation by pre-pulse inhibition (PPI) provide objective indices of sensory reactivity and sensorimotor gating that are altered in several neurodevelopmental conditions. While Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is often associated with acoustic hyper-reactivity and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) with attention-dependent gating deficits, direct transdiagnostic comparisons across development are rare. This study examined startle amplitude and PPI in 154 children and adults with ADHD (n = 47), ASD (n = 43), and typically developing controls (TD; n = 64). Startle amplitude (across 65–105 dB stimuli) and percent PPI (at 60ms and 120ms SOAs) were analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with diagnostic group, age group, sex, and their interactions as predictors. In children, no significant group differences emerged in either startle amplitude or PPI (all ps ≥ .21). In adults, however, a significant Group × Age interaction was observed for both measures (ps ≤ .037): ADHD adults showed markedly lower startle amplitude and PPI than both ASD and TD adults, with ADHD showing a significant ~59% reduction in startle amplitude from childhood to adulthood. ASD adults showed elevated startle amplitude relative to TD adults, but intact PPI comparable to TD. Group differences in the startle intensity-response function were most pronounced at higher stimulus intensities (95–105 dB). Sensitivity analyses confirmed that ADHD-related effects were robust to an age imbalance in the ASD adult subsample, though the ASD-TD startle amplitude difference was attenuated to a trend when continuous age was covaried. These findings indicate that sensory reactivity and gating abnormalities in ADHD and ASD are absent in childhood but diverge substantially by adulthood, pointing to distinct neurodevelopmental profiles that may reflect differential maturation of underlying regulatory circuits.

title: Reactivity, Habituation, and Initial Value Dependence of the Acoustic Startle Response in a Transdiagnostic Developmental Sample of ADHD and ASD
description: The acoustic startle response (ASR) serves as a reliable, non-invasive index of central nervous system function, providing an objective tool to assess sensorimotor integration and behavioral plasticity. A key feature of this reflex is its modulation across repeated stimulation, specifically through habituation, which refers to the decline in response magnitude when a startling stimulus is presented multiple times. Initial Value Dependence (IVD) which is the tendency for larger initial responses to predict steeper habituation slopes has recently been proposed as a meaningful marker of physiological flexibility yet has rarely been examined in developmental or clinical populations. This study examined eyeblink electromyographic startle habituation and IVD in 165 individuals (93 children, 72 adults) across three diagnostic groups: typically developing (TD; n = 63), Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD; n = 50), and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD; n = 52). A three-level hierarchical linear model (trials nested within blocks within participants; 318 valid blocks) was used to model habituation slopes, IVD, and their moderation by diagnosis and age group. IVD was robustly present across the full sample (B = −16.10, p < .001), replicating and extending prior findings to a developmentally and diagnostically diverse clinical sample. While overall habituation slopes did not differ by diagnosis or age, follow-up correlational analyses revealed distinct developmental trajectories of IVD: TD individuals showed strong IVD in childhood that was absent in adulthood, ASD individuals showed stable IVD across both age groups, and ADHD individuals showed an absent IVD relationship at all ages. Additionally, a significant diagnosis × age interaction emerged for baseline startle magnitude, driven by ADHD adults showing markedly lower baseline reactivity than ADHD children which was not observed in TD or ASD. These results suggest that IVD captures distinct, diagnosis-specific p

title: Tendencies that help you bend but not break. A prospective co-twin control study of personality factors predicting resilience outcomes.
description: Personality traits may affect the likelihood of demonstrating better mental health than expected given the stressor load (i.e., resilience). However, both personality and resilience are partly heritable, and the relationship may be influenced by confounding genetics and varying definitions of resilience. We examine prospective associations between the five factor personality traits, including their facets, and two different types of outcome-based resilience, while controlling for confounding from genetic and shared environmental factors. We applied linear mixed models and a co-twin control design using a Norwegian Twin Registry subsample born between 1945-60 (N = 1,516 in 2010; N = 1,987 in 2016). Personality was measured in 2010 via the NEO-PI-R, and resilience-outcomes in 2016 as the standardised regression residual of anxiety and depressive symptoms (type 1) and life satisfaction (type 2), respectively, after lifetime cumulative adversity had been regressed out. Neuroticism and most of its facets showed significant prospective associations with both resilience types after the co-twin control. Additionally, the extraversion facet “positive emotion” was significantly related to resilience type 1, and conscientiousness and its facet “achievement striving” were significantly related to resilience type 2. We discuss the utility of genetically sensitive designs and personality traits for future efforts to promote resilience.

title: The recipe for intelligence in natural and artificial systems
description: Ten years ago, two landmark papers discussed open problems in AI research from the standpoint of neuroscience and cognitive science. Today, many of these problems have been solved, mostly without direct inspiration from the study of the human brain. In this paper, we consider what is still shared between the twin endeavours of understanding natural brains and building artificial ones. We argue that as well as understanding the “ingredients” of intelligence (its component parts) both fields are now coming to grasp the “recipe” for intelligence (how they fit together). Surprisingly, this recipe has many parallels in biological and artificial systems. Despite rapid progress in AI research, there are many computational problems that remain unsolved. We discuss how these problems may be addressed in natural intelligence.

title: Predicting Early Non-Response to Buprenorphine Treatment for Opioid Use Disorder: A Machine Learning Approach
description:
Objective: Early non-response to buprenorphine treatment for opioid use disorder (OUD), including elevated craving and illicit opioid use in the first weeks of treatment, robustly predicts later recovery outcomes. Yet, little is known about who is at risk of early non-response, and identifying predictors could help classify at-risk individuals even earlier in care. We trained and tested risk prediction models for early non-response to buprenorphine treatment.  
Method: Data were from two harmonized clinical trials of buprenorphine treatment for OUD (N=562). We used elastic net and random forest models to predict two previously-validated definitions of early non-response: 1) 2+ days of opioid use in the first three weeks, and 2) a score of 2+ on a 0-10 craving visual analog scale in the first week. Predictors included pre-treatment demographics and clinical variables (e.g., polysubstance use, co-occurring disorders). 
Results: Models demonstrated weak predictive performance for both outcomes, with areas under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUCs; 0.590–0.656) below the benchmark for acceptable performance (AUC>0.7). The strongest predictors differed by the two outcome definitions; opioid use severity indicators (e.g., heroin use frequency) were strong predictors of non-response based on opioid use, while craving, withdrawal, and psychosocial functioning were strong predictors of non-response based on craving. 
Conclusions: Pre-treatment characteristics showed limited predictive value for early non-response to buprenorphine, aligning with prior work using machine learning models to predict later outcomes in OUD treatment. There is likely more value in attending to medication response in the first weeks after initiation as a prognostic marker.

title: What Gets Retrieved? Testing Effects in the Absence of Prior Study
description: Existing accounts of testing effects assume a dedicated study trial is necessary. Students must encode material before practice can strengthen memory. We tested this assumption by removing the study trial entirely. Across two experiments using prequestions and postquestions formats, in addition to clear effects of adding practice to a passage, a Practice with Feedback Only condition, in which participants answered questions about never studied material and received feedback, matched the accuracy of full instruction conditions that included a study passage. The study trial added time but not learning. These findings challenge the foundational assumption that a study trial is a necessary precondition for practice-based learning. We propose that the functional unit of learning is the generation of a response and its precise resolution through targeted feedback, not retrieval from a prior study event.

title: The Face of ChatGPT:  Using Reverse Correlation to Explore Mental Representations of AI
description: People readily attribute mental states to large language models, but it remains unclear what mental representation users hold of these systems when they personify them. Across two experiments, we used reverse correlation, a data-driven method originally developed in psychophysics, to visualize the perceptual content of users' mental representation of ChatGPT. In Phase 1, participants completed a reverse correlation task selecting which of two noisy faces looked most like ChatGPT were it human; in Phase 2, naïve participants rated the resulting classification images (CIs). Across both a female base image (Experiment 1) and a male base image (Experiment 2) image, ChatGPT CIs were perceived as warmer, more empathic, and more human-like than the base image, but did not differ reliably on competence or knowledge. By visualizing the human face people implicitly associate with ChatGPT, this study provides a novel window into the mental representations underlying contemporary Human-AI interaction. These representations may help explain how users come to trust, rely on, and socially engage with artificial agents.

title: Beyond search: solution-driven representation in human problem solving
description: Cognitive science has often characterized problem solving as search or planning within a well-defined representation. Recent accounts extend this view by treating representation construction itself as search over possible construals, guided by downstream plan utility or collisions during simulation. Yet when relevant features, actions, and constraints are unbounded, this bilevel search over representations and solutions may be computationally intractable. Drawing on field studies of naturalistic decision-making and design, we propose that people instead adopt solution-driven strategies: familiar candidate solutions are retrieved early and used to determine which problem features must be represented. Across three preregistered experiments spanning spatial navigation, abstract compositional reasoning, and cost-based itinerary planning, increasing representational demands increased reuse of familiar but suboptimal solutions. This reuse was not blind perseveration: participants selectively represented information needed to evaluate candidate solutions. Process tracing further showed that information search was centered on cached routes, yet less ordered along the route itself, consistent with holistic evaluation rather than biased incremental search. These findings suggest that solution-driven cognition is central to problem solving under large-world representational complexity.

title: Beyond Aggregate Accuracy: A Bayesian Time-Varying Autoregressive Model and Its Multilevel Extension
description: Autoregressive (AR) models are widely used to study within-person dynamics in intensive longitudinal data (ILD), but they assume constant parameters, an assumption that may not hold when the dynamics change, as during development, intervention, or recovery. The time-varying autoregressive (TV-AR) model accommodates such change by representing the intercept and AR coefficient as smooth functions of time. As originally proposed, however, it was developed for a single individual and evaluated with time-averaged accuracy metrics. We address these limitations by extending it to a Bayesian multilevel formulation with population-average and individual-specific time-varying parameters, and by emphasizing pointwise evaluation of accuracy rather than aggregate accuracy alone. Two simulation studies examined how well the single-level and multilevel models recover time-varying dynamics. Examining bias at each time point, we found that it was largest near the series boundaries and in regions of steep change or high curvature. The time-varying intercept and AR coefficient showed complementary biases that largely cancelled in the implied mean, which was therefore recovered with smaller bias. In the multilevel model, the best allocation between persons and time points depended on the parameter and inferential level: the AR coefficient favored longer series at both levels, whereas the implied mean favored more persons for population-average recovery but longer series for individual recovery. We discuss implications and recommendations for study design, result interpretation, and model selection.

title: Examining Linking Language and Causal Implications in Observational Psychological Capital Research
description: The present study examines whether the issues with implied causal inference identified by Haber et al. (2022) in observational health research extend to the field of industrial-organisational (IO) psychology. Haber et al. found that causal relationships were implied through the language used to link exposures and outcomes and through recommendations for action. This small-scale conceptual replication applies Haber et al.’s approach to psychological capital (PsyCap), an increasingly popular construct in the domain of IO psychology. Specifically, we evaluate the causal strength of the language used to describe the nature of the connection between given variables when reporting findings and when making recommendations for action in 50 PsyCap articles. The majority of action recommendations were found to have strong causal implications (85% in abstracts, 84% in discussion sections), and most linking sentences implied some level of causality (64% in abstracts, 58% in discussion sections). This was despite very few articles explicitly stating an intention to estimate causal effects (10%) and many explicitly warning against drawing causal inferences (42%). No significant relationship was found between the causality implied in the linking sentences and the strength of causal implication of the action recommendations. These findings have important implications for how research is implemented, and very real consequences for those who are the focus of such implementations. Increased transparency about causal interests and reasoning is needed to uphold the credibility of findings and the integrity of applications of observational PsyCap research.

title: Using a Master Narrative Framework to Examine Conversations about Race and Ethnicity
description:
Objective. Systemic racism, reinforced by color-evasive ideology, shapes the ways that emerging adults process and discuss race/ethnicity. In this preregistered and mixed methods study, we used a master narrative framework to examine narratives about race/ethnicity among U.S. emerging adult friend-pairs.
Method. Participants consisted of 107 friend-pairs (N = 214 total) who provided individually written racial/ethnic narratives and engaged in an unstructured conversation with each other in which race/ethnicity was brought up.
Results. Using quantitative methods, we found that meaning making of racial/ethnic experiences was higher in written relative to spoken narrative contexts. Additionally, using qualitative methods, we identified specific conversation dynamics capturing how young people discussed race/ethnicity including subthemes of mitigation and encouragement, finding that encouragement dynamics were related to higher meaning making.
Conclusion. Our discussion centers on the value of integrating qualitative and quantitative methods to explore how meaning of race/ethnicity is silenced or encouraged, and to reveal how master narratives perpetuating racial inequality may be disrupted.

title: From suspicion to violent action: A multi-category framework of fear speech using qualitative content analysis and machine learning
description: Current online hate speech flagging systems overlook more subtle forms of dangerous content that are not overtly violent, and more akin to what has been described as fear speech. Fear plays a crucial role in the process of radicalisation (Doosje et al., 2013), and is an important motivator of violence (Buyse, 2014). Yet, very little work has directly examined types and variations of online fear speech. In Study 1, we qualitatively content analysed a pre-annotated fear speech dataset to propose a novel framework of fear speech classification. This framework highlights a range of varying intensities of fear messages (Suspicion, Non-Violent Fear, Violent Fear, Non-Violent Call to Action, and Violent Call to Action). In Study 2, we generated synthetic data corresponding to our proposed framework, and used it to train a lightweight transformer-based model using contrastive learning, to enable automatic multilabel classification of fear speech. Our model outperformed multiple baselines, showing a decent ability to detect and classify fear speech. The proposed framework extends theorisation on hate speech and can be used to automatically detect less obvious harmful speech online, inform moderation policies and develop more appropriate counter fear-speech designs.

title: A Cultural-Ecosocial Framework for Brain Health Research
description:
There has been enormous investment in neuroscience research to advance our knowledge of
neurological and psychiatric disorders and to identify the mechanisms that promote healthy
neurodevelopment and brain functioning. However, despite advances, much of this research has
focused on nonrepresentative populations, clinical samples, or laboratory analogues of real-world
settings. The application of neuroscience to brain health can be greatly enhanced by systematic
integration of contextual factors into research to ensure that findings are representative of the relevant
populations, and that they capture relevant variables of the environment and social-cultural context.
This contextual information should inform the design of studies, strengthen methodological rigour,
enrich the interpretation of results, and guide the translation of scientific findings into policy and
practice. The aim of this paper is to provide key principles for an integrative framework for brain health
research. This is motivated by the concern that to advance knowledge translation in clinical, public
health and policy domains, neuroscientific studies on brain disorders and mental health need to
incorporate measures of structural and social determinants of health in research to contribute to real-
world impact at scale.

title: The trade-off between cooperation and intention-based fairness
description: Groups frequently ostracise members who are unable, rather than unwilling, to contribute to collective efforts, despite many people viewing such exclusions as fundamentally unfair. Across two experiments (N=520), we demonstrate that this apparent contradiction arises because, under ambiguity, when individuals cannot distinguish between intentional free-riding and genuine inability, groups face a dilemma: be tolerant and risk cooperation breaking down, or be intolerant and risk ostracising individuals who are unable to contribute and, arguably, deserve help. We show that depending on the economic costs of tolerance, groups adopt different strategies to address this dilemma. They either act in line with intention-based fairness ideals but risk cooperation decline or they adopt a more rigid stance and ostracize non-contributing group members, even when they know that these group members were unable to contribute. Our findings can elucidate why groups often act intolerantly towards vulnerable members, despite widespread norms endorsing intention-based fairness.

title: Sociocultural Correlates of Infant Visual Attention
description: Infant visual attention—most often operationalized through looking behavior—has long served as a foundational method and explanatory construct in the study of early cognitive, perceptual, and learning processes. Despite extensive evidence that attention is plastic, experience-dependent, and embedded in structured environments, infant looking time is implicitly regarded as a culturally neutral measure. This review critically examines that assumption. Synthesizing evidence from cross-national, cross-linguistic, socioeconomic, and social-ecological research, infants’ visual attention varies systematically with culturally embedded experiences. Importantly, these differences do not map cleanly onto reified cultural categories (e.g. WEIRD/non-WEIRD), nor do they break down systematically by socioeconomic factors. Instead, the literature points to variability in experience-dependent tuning of distinct components of visual attention. Building on these findings, an acculturated model of infant visual attention is advanced in which endogenous attentional mechanisms interact dynamically with culturally structured experience. This approach strengthens the interpretive power and generalizability of attention-based methods in a continually diversifying developmental science.

title: How to Implement Interdisciplinary Care in Persistent Pain: A Process-Based Framework for Clinical Decision-Making
description:
Interdisciplinary care is widely recognised as the gold standard for the management of persistent pain. However, despite strong theoretical support, its implementation remains inconsistent due to the lack of operational frameworks that guide how multidisciplinary information should be integrated into coordinated clinical decision-making. Existing biopsychosocial, process-based, and network-informed models provide valuable conceptual foundations but offer limited guidance on how clinicians should identify, prioritise, and target processes within interdisciplinary practice.

This paper builds on the previously proposed Process-Based Interdisciplinary Therapy (PbIT) framework by operationalising its application to interdisciplinary clinical decision-making in persistent pain. PbIT integrates the Pain and Movement Reasoning Model (PMRM), micro-level functional analysis, and the Extended Evolutionary Meta-Model (EEMM) into a recursive clinical decision-making system that supports shared formulation, process prioritisation, intervention selection, and ongoing treatment adaptation. Rather than prescribing discipline-specific interventions, the framework provides a flexible structure for coordinating professional expertise around modifiable biopsychosocial processes identified through interdisciplinary assessment.

To facilitate implementation, PbIT is operationalised through a six-step clinical workflow encompassing interdisciplinary assessment, network formulation, functional analysis, target prioritisation, intervention design, continuous monitoring, and readjustment. A hypothetical clinical case is used throughout to illustrate how the framework may support collaborative reasoning and adaptive treatment planning.

By making interdisciplinary clinical reasoning explicit, transparent, and reproducible, PbIT offers a practical framework for translating process-based and network-informed theory into coordinated interdisciplinary care. The framework has the potential to impr

title: “We love our jobs!” Systems Thinking and Participatory Action Research for the future of neurodiversity and skilled employment
description: Although neurodivergent people can achieve high levels of academic and vocational training in the same professions as others, they remain un- and under-employed; this is in part due to the “wickedness” of the problem. To address the complexity we convened a group of neurodivergent lived experience and employment experts to  1) define which problems in neurodiversity and skilled employment are most urgent to address; 2) create actionable strategies with potential leverage for generating the desired employment outcomes. We used qualitative methods from the systems thinking field: Soft Systems Methodology, futures visioning and SWOT analysis, and Group Model Building (system dynamics). The workshops resulted in identifying intervention leverage between organizational psychology constructs of employee wellbeing, workplace climate, and mutually beneficial workplace relationships, in conjunction with a new disability stress construct, rather than specific skills building or remediation of disability characteristics. Reaching a future in which neurodivergent people love their jobs will take education, resources, champions from the outside and inside, universal access, paradigm shifts, infrastructure, and recognition of individual and social complexity. We recommend examining the organizational psychology literature for adaptable interventions; focusing on peer support; leveraging emerging technology to manage complexity; and supporting supervisor behavior change based on authentic understanding.
